p1: sw0590 is a Jawa minifigure released in 2014 as part of the Star Wars Classic Trilogy theme. The defining feature of this variant is the highly detailed 'Gold Badge' printed on its bandolier, perfectly capturing the vintage, rugged aesthetic of the iconic Tatooine scrap scavengers.
p2: Featured exclusively in the massive UCS Sandcrawler (75059) and the Droid Escape Pod (75136), this character is an essential piece for any Star Wars diorama. With its accessible market value, it remains consistently popular among fans looking to army-build Jawas for sprawling desert scenes or droid auction displays.

p1: sw0001a is a classic Battle Droid variant from the early days of LEGO Star Wars, introduced in 1999 alongside Episode 1: The Phantom Menace. This specific version is distinguished by its tan color, angled arms, and a 1x2 plate on its back, representing the Trade Federation's standard infantry.
p2: Found in vintage sets like the Naboo Swamp (7121) and the massive MTT (75428), this figure is a nostalgic piece of LEGO history. It's highly sought after by collectors looking to recreate the original 1999 Trade Federation army or those who appreciate the charm of the earliest LEGO Star Wars designs.

p1: sw0001b is the quintessential Tan Battle Droid, featuring the classic angled arms that define the early LEGO Star Wars era. Introduced in 1999, this figure became the backbone of countless Trade Federation and Separatist armies built by fans worldwide.
p2: Appearing in a massive array of sets over the years, from the original Trade Federation MTT (7184) to the Clone Turbo Tank (7675), this droid is the ultimate army-building unit. Its simple yet iconic design makes it a staple for any prequel-era diorama or large-scale battle scene.

p1: sw0001c represents a slight evolution in the design of the standard Tan Battle Droid, introduced in 2007. This variant is notable for featuring one angled arm and one straight arm, allowing it to hold its blaster rifle in a more realistic, forward-aiming pose.
p2: This updated design became the new standard, appearing in dozens of sets including the massive Trade Federation MTT (7662) and the AT-TE (75019). It is an essential figure for army builders looking for a more dynamic and battle-ready Separatist force.

p1: sw0001d is a specific variant of the Tan Battle Droid introduced during The Clone Wars era in 2008. Unlike previous versions, this droid features two straight arms, giving it a distinct, rigid posture.
p2: Found in sets like the Droid Gunship (7678) and the Clone Trooper & Battle Droid Battle Pack (75372), this variant offers a unique posing option for collectors. It's a great piece for adding variety to a large Separatist droid army display.

p1: sw0002 is the legendary, original Boba Fett minifigure released in 2000. This highly coveted figure features the classic old gray color palette for his Mandalorian armor, a design that predates the shift to modern bluish-gray tones.
p2: Included in iconic early sets like the Slave I (7144) and the Millennium Falcon (7190), this figure is a holy grail for vintage LEGO Star Wars collectors. Its classic, unrefined charm and historical significance make it one of the most valuable and recognizable minifigures from the original trilogy line.

p1: sw0002a is the updated 2006 version of the classic Boba Fett minifigure. This variant marks the transition to the modern 'bluish gray' color palette for his armor, offering a slightly updated look while retaining the charm of the original design.
p2: Featured in major sets like the Slave I (6209) and Jabba's Sail Barge (6210), this figure represents a key era in LEGO Star Wars history. It remains a highly desirable piece for collectors looking to complete their mid-2000s original trilogy displays.

p1: sw0002b is a rare and specific variant of Boba Fett released in 2009. While it uses the bluish-gray armor, its defining feature is the distinct dark red highlights on the helmet, setting it apart from previous iterations.
p2: This unique figure was exclusively available in the Star Wars Magnet Set (852552), making it a relatively uncommon find today. It's a prized variant for dedicated Boba Fett collectors and completists who seek out every subtle difference in the bounty hunter's LEGO history.

p1: sw0003 is the original, iconic Darth Maul minifigure from the inaugural 1999 LEGO Star Wars wave. This classic design features his menacing red and black facial tattoos, a black hood and cape, and a simple printed sash without a pouch.
p2: Appearing in legendary sets like the Sith Infiltrator (7151) and Lightsaber Duel (7101), this figure is a cornerstone of any vintage LEGO Star Wars collection. Its historical significance as the first representation of the fearsome Sith Lord makes it a highly treasured and nostalgic piece.

p1: sw0004 is the very first iteration of the legendary Darth Vader minifigure, released in 1999. This vintage classic is easily identifiable by its old light gray head piece underneath the iconic helmet, a hallmark of the earliest LEGO Star Wars sets.
p2: Featured in foundational sets like the TIE Fighter & Y-wing (7150) and the original Cloud City (10123), this figure is a piece of LEGO history. It remains a highly sought-after collectible for fans looking to own the original brick-built version of the galaxy's most feared Sith Lord.

p1: sw0004a is a slightly updated version of the classic Darth Vader minifigure, introduced in 2004. The key difference in this variant is the use of a modern light bluish-gray head piece beneath the helmet, reflecting LEGO's color palette shift during that era.
p2: Found in sets like the TIE Collection (10131) and the TIE Fighter & Y-wing re-release (7262), this figure bridges the gap between the vintage 1999 design and later, more detailed versions. It's a great piece for collectors tracking the subtle evolutions of iconic characters.

p1: sw0005 is the original Imperial Scout Trooper minifigure, released in 1999. This classic figure is notable for its vintage yellow head piece hidden beneath the helmet and its simple, dark gray torso printing, characteristic of the earliest LEGO Star Wars designs.
p2: Included in early sets like Speeder Bikes (7128) and the Ewok Attack (7139), this figure is a nostalgic favorite. It's an essential piece for collectors looking to build a vintage Endor diorama or complete their 1999 original trilogy lineup.

p1: sw0005a is an updated version of the Imperial Scout Trooper, introduced in 2009. This variant features a plain black head piece to better simulate the dark visor of the helmet, along with updated dark bluish-gray torso printing.
p2: Appearing in sets like the Battle of Endor (8038) and the Ewok Attack (7956), this figure offers a more movie-accurate look than its yellow-headed predecessor. It remains a popular choice for army builders creating detailed forest moon battle scenes.

p1: sw0005b is a further refined Imperial Scout Trooper variant released in 2012. While it retains the dark bluish-gray torso print, it features a printed black head beneath the helmet, adding a subtle layer of detail for collectors.
p2: Found exclusively in the Endor Rebel Trooper & Imperial Trooper Battle Pack (9489), this figure was designed specifically for army building. It's an excellent addition for fans looking to amass a highly detailed Imperial scouting force.

p1: sw0006 is the unique and highly specialized Aldar Beedo minifigure, released in 2000. This figure captures the distinct, multi-limbed look of the Glymphid podracer pilot from Episode 1, utilizing a custom molded piece for his head and torso.
p2: Exclusive to the Podracing Bucket (7159) set, this figure is a rare and fascinating piece of early LEGO Star Wars design. It is highly prized by collectors for its unique mold and its representation of the thrilling Boonta Eve Classic podrace.

p1: sw0007 is the original young Anakin Skywalker minifigure from 1999, depicting him in his podracing gear. This classic variant features a brown aviator cap and the vintage yellow skin tone, perfectly capturing his appearance in Episode 1.
p2: Included in the iconic Naboo Fighter (7141) set, this figure is a nostalgic cornerstone for fans of The Phantom Menace. Its simple, classic design makes it a beloved piece for those collecting the very first wave of LEGO Star Wars characters.

p1: sw0008 is another early variant of young Anakin Skywalker, also released in 1999. This version is distinguished by its light gray aviator cap, representing a different look from his podracing adventures in Episode 1.
p2: Found in classic sets like Anakin's Podracer (7131) and the Mos Espa Podrace (7171), this figure is a key piece for recreating the Boonta Eve Classic. It remains a highly nostalgic and collectible item for fans of the original LEGO Star Wars prequel sets.

p1: sw0009 is the classic Biggs Darklighter minifigure, introduced in 1999. This vintage figure features the iconic orange Rebel pilot jumpsuit and a yellow head with Biggs' signature mustache, capturing his look from the Battle of Yavin.
p2: Included in the original X-wing Fighter (7140) and its re-release (7142), this figure is a must-have for fans of Red Squadron. Its classic design and historical significance make it a treasured piece for collectors building a vintage Rebel Alliance display.

p1: sw0010 is the original C-3PO minifigure, released in 2000. This classic variant is molded in pearl light gold, giving the protocol droid a distinct, slightly muted metallic sheen compared to later, brighter versions.
p2: Appearing in iconic early sets like the Millennium Falcon (7190) and Jabba's Message (4475), this figure is a foundational piece of LEGO Star Wars history. Its simple, elegant design makes it a highly nostalgic and essential collectible for fans of the original trilogy.

p1: sw0011 is the very first Chewbacca minifigure, introduced in 2000. This vintage classic is molded in a solid, older brown color, utilizing a unique single-piece head and torso mold to capture the Wookiee's furry appearance.
p2: Found in early sets like the Millennium Falcon (7190) and the Imperial AT-ST (7127), this figure is a true piece of LEGO Star Wars heritage. Its classic, unrefined color and mold make it a highly prized collectible for fans of the original 2000 wave.

p1: sw0011a is the updated Chewbacca minifigure, introduced in 2004. This variant transitioned the Wookiee to the modern 'reddish brown' color palette, providing a warmer and more accurate tone for his fur while retaining the classic head/torso mold.
p2: This version became the standard Chewbacca for many years, appearing in countless sets from the Millennium Falcon (4504) to the massive Death Star (10188). It is an essential and ubiquitous figure for any collector building a comprehensive Star Wars display.

p1: sw0012 is the original Dak Ralter minifigure, released in 1999. As Luke Skywalker's brave tailgunner during the Battle of Hoth, this figure is a key part of the early Rebel Alliance lineup, featuring the classic old gray hips and a yellow head.
p2: Found exclusively in the original Snowspeeder (7130), this figure is a nostalgic gem for vintage collectors. It captures the simple yet effective design language of the first wave of LEGO Star Wars, making it a prized piece for Hoth-themed dioramas.

p1: sw0012a is the 2004 update of Dak Ralter, reflecting LEGO's shift to the modern bluish-gray color palette. This version maintains the classic yellow head but updates the hips to dark bluish gray, aligning with the aesthetic of the mid-2000s Star Wars sets.
p2: Featured in the Snowspeeder (4500), this variant is a great example of the subtle color transitions that define different eras of LEGO collecting. It's an essential figure for fans looking to complete their 2004-era Rebel pilot collection.

p1: sw0012b represents a significant milestone for Dak Ralter, released in 2007. This was the first version of the character to feature a 'light nougat' flesh-toned head, moving away from the classic yellow skin as LEGO transitioned to more realistic character representations.
p2: Found in the Hoth Rebel Base (7666), this figure is highly valued for its improved detail and modern look. It's a must-have for collectors who prefer the flesh-toned era of LEGO Star Wars and want a more realistic Dak for their Hoth displays.

p1: sw0013 is the classic Gungan Soldier, introduced in 2000. This figure captures the unique look of the Naboo natives with its custom molded head and vibrant green outfit, representing the brave warriors who fought in the Battle of Naboo.
p2: Featured in the Gungan Patrol (7115), this figure is a staple for any Episode 1 collection. Its unique mold and historical place as one of the first non-humanoid LEGO Star Wars figures make it a fascinating piece for army builders and prequel fans.

p1: sw0014 is a legendary version of Han Solo, released in 2000. This iconic figure features the classic yellow head and his signature black vest over a tan shirt, along with blue legs detailed with his famous smuggler's belts.
p2: Exclusively found in the original Millennium Falcon (7190), this figure is a cornerstone of any vintage Star Wars collection. Its high market value and association with one of the most famous LEGO sets of all time make it a true crown jewel for serious collectors.

p1: sw0015 is a classic Han Solo variant from 2000, depicting him in his simple white shirt without wrinkles. This version features brown legs and the vintage yellow head, capturing his rugged look during the rescue of Princess Leia.
p2: Found in the Desert Skiff (7104), this figure is a great example of the early, minimalist design approach to LEGO Star Wars characters. It's an essential piece for fans of the original trilogy who appreciate the charm of the first-generation Han Solo minifigures.

p1: sw0016 is the original Hoth Rebel Trooper, released in 1999. This vintage figure is iconic for its yellow head and the distinct brown visor piece on its helmet, representing the brave soldiers who defended Echo Base.
p2: Included in the classic Snowspeeder (7130), this figure is a nostalgic favorite for many long-time collectors. Its simple yet effective design makes it a foundational piece for any Hoth battle display and a key part of the first wave of LEGO Star Wars.

p1: sw0017 is the original Jar Jar Binks minifigure, released in 1999. This figure is famous for its unique custom-molded head, which was a groundbreaking design for LEGO at the time, perfectly capturing the clumsy Gungan's expressive face.
p2: Appearing in many early Episode 1 sets like the Gungan Sub (7161) and Naboo Swamp (7121), Jar Jar is a must-have for prequel fans. His unique mold and historical significance as one of the most recognizable characters from the first wave of LEGO Star Wars make him a staple for any collection.

p1: sw0018 is the classic Luke Skywalker in his Endor camouflage outfit, released in 1999. This vintage figure features the iconic green and brown camo print on the torso and a yellow head, representing Luke during the mission to the forest moon.
p2: Included in the original Speeder Bikes (7128) set, this figure is a nostalgic favorite for fans of Return of the Jedi. Its simple, classic design makes it a foundational piece for any Endor-themed diorama and a key part of the first wave of LEGO Star Wars.

p1: sw0019 is the original Luke Skywalker in his X-wing Pilot suit, released in 1999. This vintage classic features the iconic orange flight suit with a simple torso print, old gray hips, and a yellow head with the classic Rebel pilot helmet.
p2: Found in foundational sets like the original X-wing Fighter (7140) and Snowspeeder (7130), this figure is a piece of LEGO history. It remains a highly sought-after collectible for fans looking to own the original brick-built version of the galaxy's greatest pilot.

p1: sw0019a is the 2004 update of Luke Skywalker's Pilot suit, reflecting LEGO's shift to the modern bluish-gray color palette. This version maintains the classic yellow head and simple torso print but updates the hips to dark bluish gray.

p2: Appearing in foundational sets like the original Landspeeder (7110) and Millennium Falcon (7190), this figure is a piece of LEGO history. It remains a highly nostalgic and essential collectible for fans of the original trilogy and the very first wave of LEGO Star Wars.

p1: sw0022 is the classic Naboo Security Officer, released in 2000. This figure captures the look of the Naboo Royal Security Forces with its brown uniform, yellow head, and the distinct police-style hat, representing the brave defenders of Theed.
